coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence mechanism and optoelectronic responses of Janus pyrene and Janus coronene QDs for OLEDs & nanomedical applications

Drissi, L.B.
Assad, R.
Fritzsche, W.

First principles
Graphene quantum dots
Excitonic properties
Photoluminescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the “Académie Hassan II des Sciences et Techniques”-Morocco for its financial support. The authors also thank the LPHE-MS, Faculty of Sciences, Mohammed V University in Rabat, Morocco for the technical support through computer facilities, where all the calculations have been performed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top and side views of the structurally optimized pristine and Janus QDs functionalized with NH2, OH, CHO and COOH from one side, and with hydrogen atom from the opposite side.

Top and side views of the HOMO and LUMO energies for pristine and Janus
C
16
H
10
.

Top and side views of spatial distribution of the frontier orbitals for pristine and janus
C
24
H
12
.

Absorption spectra corresponding to pristine and Janus (a)
C
16
H
10
and (b)
C
24
H
12
configurations, for the incident light polarized along the x-axis. H represents the spectra of pristine GQDs, while OH, NH2, CHO and COOH refer to the spectra curves of Janus CQDs.

Percentage (%) of molecular dopants in Janus CQDs surface
C
16
H
10
and
C
24
H
12
.

Calculated parameters for pristine and Janus QDs: maximum deformation angles α and β (in degree°), binding energy
E
B
, hardness η and electrophilicity ω all in (eV).
E
g
G G A
and
E
g
G W
are respectively the HOMO–LUMO energy gap in (eV) obtained by GGA and GW approximations.
E
o p t
X
and
E
b
X
are respectively the optical gap and the exciton binding energy.
Δ
S − T
X
represents the singlet–triplet energy splitting and it is dimensionless.

TDDFT calculated parameters: the absorption and emission energies in (eV)
E
a b s
and
E
e m
and their corresponding absorption and emission wavelengths in (nm)
λ
a b s
and
λ
e m
. The Stokes shift is in (eV).

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence mechanism and optoelectronic responses of Janus pyrene and Janus coronene QDs for OLEDs & nanomedical applications

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request

ce:section.ce:para: Janus materials are employed to describe asymmetric nanostructures whose both surfaces are of distinct physical and chemical properties [1]. Interestingly, these promising nanoparticles have been widely investigated and attracted intensive interest from various research fields for use in a broad variety of potential applications such as nanoelectronics, catalysis, actuators, surfactant, photocatalytic, thermoelectric and thermoelastic [2–9], besides nanomedical diagnostic/ therapeutic applications like biosensor, drug delivery, molecular imaging, biomolecular labelling and cancer treatment [10–14].

The particularity of these nanoparticles raised from the unique anisotropic features they exhibit including the small size, the biocompatibility, the efficient stimuli-responsive character, the significant optoelectronic and magnetic behaviour, and the interesting surface properties such as hydrophobicity, charge and polarity [15,16]. Over the past three decades, Janus nanoparticles have been progressively developed due to the great advancement of synthesis methods, which led to the fabrication of a large number of novel Janus nanomaterials.

Two-dimensional (2D) Janus nanomaterials are among the many Janus structures that acquired increasing regard especially after the discovery of graphene in 2004 [17–20]. Indeed, this nanomaterial has presented a main building block to synthesize 2D Janus nanomaterial through surface-functionalization by mean of hydrogen/halogens, metal/metal oxide, small molecules or polymers [21,22]. In the context of overcoming graphene drawbacks and tuning its properties, hydrogenation mechanisms was mainly a pioneer indication to the study of 2D Janus nanomaterials properties. It has been shown that half and full hydrogenation leading to graphane and graphone respectively alter drastically the electronic, magnetic, topological and transport properties of graphene 2D sheet [23]. At this point, many theoretical studies have shown that the full hydrogenation of graphene sheet was expected to show peculiar properties such as band gap widening and ferromagnetism [24,25]. In like manner, half-hydrogenation was found to break the delocalized π -bonding network of graphene resulting in localized and unpaired electrons in the unhydrogenated carbon atoms, which leads to a ferromagnetic semiconductor with a small indirect gap [26].

Apart from hydrogenation, halogenation sounds an interesting approach for generating new graphene-based derivatives with novel physical and chemical properties [27,28]. On the basis of first-principles calculations, a study of fluorinated graphene was performed showing that the band gap of graphene sheet can vary considerably as a function of fluorination coverage and configuration [29]. In parallel, chlorination reaction of graphene has been widely investigated to examine the effect behind the interaction of chlorine atoms with graphene. This new configuration has resulted in a tunable band gap falling into a range from 0 to
1 . 3
eV
[30]. Interestingly, rather than single-atom surface decoration, the asymmetric functionalization using either hydrogen/halogen, different halogen atoms (F, Cl, Br...) or hydrogen/oxygen-functional groups on each side, sounds potential leading to a variety of Janus graphene derivatives. Therefore, a whole range of succeeded configurations was designed like hydrofluorinated graphene (HFG) structures with a bandgap ranging between 3.34 and
5 . 25
eV
[31], Janus-type mosaic graphene by means of halogens, showing a significant band gap opening [32]. Additionally, an experimental study of Janus graphene obtained via decorating each side by X (halogen groups) and Y (aryl or oxygen-functional groups), has demonstrated that the functionalities on one side affects the chemical reactivity and the surface wettability of the opposite side revealing a kind of a remarkable communication between X and Y functionalities [33].

In this paper, we aim to establish a link between the foregoing Janus graphene studies and our previous works about manipulating the optoelectronic and photoluminescence properties of graphene and graphene-like quantum dots . Explicitly, after investigating the impact of morphological [34] as well as chemical modification (substitution, edge functionalization) [13,35] on graphene-based quantum dots, now we look forward to study double sided surface functionalization of the well known diamond-shaped and hexagonal graphene quantum dots (CQDs), referred to as pyrene and coronene, examining the effects of both basal plan decoration and shape variation. Our strategy in designing Janus Quantum dots (JQDs) is based on utilizing hydrogen atoms on one side of the carbon basal plan and organic molecular functional groups on the other side, in order to involve the resulted derivatives in nanomedical applications. Our findings sound promising given the novel features characterizing the investigated JQDs. Firstly, surface-functionalization is found to break the planarity of graphene structure by introducing remarkable distortions originating mainly from the weight and the rigidity of the functional groups. Additionally, all JQDs have shown a considerable energetic and chemical stability confirmed through computing the binding energy and the global reactivity indices. Concerning the optoelectronic properties, the energy gap and the optical gap were found to be strongly dependent on the shape and the functional-group type. Accurately, the energy gap, the optical gap and the exciton binding energy values vary in opposite directions depending on the JQDs shape, and this is explained by the reordering of the orbitals and the creation of hybridization. From the other hand, calculations of the energy splitting of excited states have demonstrated that our structures exhibit a photoluminescent nature confirmed by TDDFT calculations of the emission, showing that JQDs present a whole range of photoluminescent nanomaterials with different colours, suitable for a new generation of applications.

Our paper is organized as follow: We start by detailing the used computational methods and describing the studied systems in Section 2, then we discuss the entire investigated properties in Section 3. We summarize our work with a conclusion.

We initiate our computations by performing ground-state density functional theory (DFT) calculations based on the plane-wave pseudopotential method. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [36], implemented in Quantum espresso (QE) simulation package [37], is employed to treat the exchange–correlation of electrons. A plane-wave basis set with kinetic energy cutoff of 60 Ry is used with a norm-conserving pseudo-potential to describe electronic wavefunctions . To eliminate the spurious interactions emerging from boundary conditions, a vacuum space between the structure and its periodic image is set in 20 Å along the three directions. All calculations are performed at the
Γ −
point.

In this work, two different GQDs shapes are investigated, namely, diamond shaped pyrene (ds-C16H10) and hexagonal coronene (h-C24H
12
)
. Pristine pyrene exhibits two diagonals with two different sizes: the larger diagonal is about 9.52 Å while the smaller diagonal is about 6.98 Å . For Pristine coronene characterized with a circular shape, the diameter value is 9.55 Å . As shown in Fig. 1, we study four Janus configurations where pristine CQDs are double-sided functionalized. More precisely, hydrogen atoms decorate one side while the opposite surface of QDs is chemically modified with functional groups, namely
f u n =
OH, CHO, COOH and NH2. The percentage
P
f u n
of the investigated organic molecular groups, in both pyrene and coronene is calculated with the following expression [38]:
(1)
P
f u n
= 100 ×
N
f u n
×
M
f u n
M
J Q D s
with
N
H
and
M
H
represent the number and the molecular mass of H-atoms, respectively, while
N
f u n
and
M
f u n
correspond to the number and the molecular mass of dopant molecules (OH, CHO, COOH and NH2 ), respectively.
M
J Q D s
is the molecular mass of Janus GQDs. The associated result is listed in Table 1.

All the Janus systems are optimized through fully relaxing the atomic structures until the remaining force are smaller than 10−3
eV / Å
. Furthermore, as GGA is known to underestimate the energy gap, we estimate the quasiparticle corrections (QP) to DFT-GGA energy gap using non-self consistent GW calculations implemented in YAMBO program suite [39]. To perform QP calculations, we replace the exchange–correlation potential V
x c
, used in the DFT, by a nonlocal, energy-dependent self-energy operator
∑
(
E
n k
Q P
)
defined as:
(2)
∑
(
r
1
,
r
2
, ω )
=
i
2 π
∫
e
i δ
ω
′
G
(
r
1
,
r
2
, ω +
ω
′
)
W
(
r
1
,
r
2
,
ω
′
)
d
ω
′
where
r
1
and
r
2
are the electron and the hole positions. The first order quasiparticle (QP) corrections are obtained using Hedin [40] GW approximation, while excitonic effects are treated by solving the Bethe–Salpeter equation (BSE) [41]. The construction of the dielectric matrix and self-energy operator of all studied systems was effectuated with a total of 600 bands. Similar to DFT method, only gamma point was considered for QP and BSE calculations. Furthermore, the photoluminescence of the investigated structures was estimated through performing time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) procedure within the B3LYP hybrid exchange–correlation density functional approximation with a 6-31G(d) basis set (B3LYP/6-31G(d)) [42].

Shape and size effect on photoluminescence mechanism and optoelectronic properties of Janus graphene quantum dots with two sizes, namely, diamond shaped pyrene (ds-C16H10) and hexagonal coronene (h-C24H
12
)
is explored and determined in this section.

Pristine graphene quantum dots (CQDs), also known as pyrene
C
16
H
10
and coronene
C
24
H
12
, exhibit a planar structure having
D
2 h
and
D
6 h
point groups symmetry, respectively [43,44]. Both nanostructures have a mixture of zigzag edges and armchair corners with hydrogen saturated dangling bonds to guarantee a maximum of stability. Notice that carbon atoms in are
s
p
2
hybridized since the bond angles are in the range of 118° and 121° [45].

As illustrated in Fig. 1, Janus configurations are obtained through the double-sided functionalization of pristine CQDs with hydrogen atoms from one side (black dots) and organic molecular groups from the opposite one (red dots). Four different functional groups, namely, hydroxyl (OH), formyl (CHO), carboxyl (COOH) and amino (NH2) are considered.

Clearly, the functionalization distorts the planarity of pristine configurations creating a kind of rippling. The accurate examination of the optimized structures indicates that two different types of distortions arise depending on the shape of CQDs. Both configurations have displayed a kind of buckled geometry extended along the whole carbon skeleton, in addition to a considerable breaking along the large diagonal observed for DSQDs. In order to quantify the distortion effects and also to unfold which functional group is responsible for the most pronounced deformation, we determine the angles α and β , which are defined as the maximum deformation of the functionalized molecular configurations with respect to pristine planar structures. Consequently, the planarity of GQDs, inherited from graphene monosheet structure, has been suppressed upon surface-doping. From the angles α and β illustrated in Fig. 1 and their values reported in Table 2, one can deduce that the functionalization has transformed the GQDs from a planar surface with a zero angle, as clearly observed in the side view plot of Fig. 1(a and f) to deformated structures with angle values that vary from 42.1 ° to 47.2 ° for pyrene, and 44.1 ° to 51.8 ° for coronene QDs. More precisely:

(i) In Janus-pyrene structures, carboxylated
C
16
H
10
have the largest α and the lowest β angles indicating that COOH molecule has a great impact on the geometry of the structures due to its large molecular weight. In particular, the symmetrical location of two COOH atoms from one face and one hydrogen atom from the opposite face induce a large deformation of the structure, however, the presence of two hydrogen atoms and one COOH has the minor effect due to the great impact of COOH compared to H-atom. Similarly, both CHO and NH2 have shown a considerable impact on CQDs, while OH exhibits a minor effect among the considered molecules.

(ii) For Janus-coronene configurations, α represents the angle between two hydrogenated carbon atoms located on the diagonal of the structure, while β is the angle between two decorated carbon atoms situated at the same diagonal. Here also, COOH group induces the most pronounced distortion, followed by CHO, NH2 and then the OH molecules. These results are due to two main factors: the group molecular weight of (COOH, CHO and OH) and the rigidity of NH2 [46].

To analyse the energetic stability and examine the feasibility of the different JCQDs described above, we first evaluate the binding energy
E
B
defined as the difference between the total energy of JCQDs (
E
J C Q D
)
and the sum of the energies of pristine CQDs (
E
C Q D
)
, functional groups (
E
f u n
)
with (
f u n
= OH, CHO, COOH, NH2), and dihydrogen (
E
H
2
)
. The energy
E
B
is expressed as follows:
(3)
E
B
=
1
N
(
E
J C Q D
−
E
C Q D
−
N
f u n
E
f u n
−
N
H
2
E
H
2
)
where
N
f u n
and
N
H
are the number of functional groups and hydrogen atoms respectively, while N is their sum. As reported in Table 2, all the obtained binding energies are negative which indicates that our JCQDs are thermodynamically stable. The more negative
E
B
denotes the strong binding of functional groups and hydrogen atoms to the carbon skeleton. Indeed, we note that coronene structures are more stable than their pyrene counterparts. Furthermore, JCQDs-OH are the most stable configurations while formyl functional (CHO) gives rise to the least stable ones for both diamond shaped C16H10 and hexagonal C24H
12
. This result is in good agreement with the stability of decorated graphenic surfaces with functional groups [47]. It is worth noting that the change in stability in term of functional group is mainly attributed to the decrease of the corresponding electronegativity (
χ
)
; since the highest value of
χ = 3 . 55
is reported for hydroxyl group, followed by
χ = 3 . 12
for amino, then
χ =
2.63 for carboxyl and finally formyl group with
χ = 2 . 60
[48]

The chemical stability is also explored through determining the global reactivity descriptor, namely the hardness η and the electrophilicity index ω expressed by [49,50]:
η =
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
2
,
and
ω =
μ
2
2 η
,
with
μ = −
E
H O M O
+
E
L U M O
2
HOMO and LUMO refer to the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals respectively. Noticeably, results listed in Table 2 show that the chemical stability is strongly related to the shape of JCQDs. Indeed, surface-functionalized coronene structures are found to be much harder than pyrene configurations, which means that they are much stable and less reactive. Moreover, surface-functionalization has also affected the hardness of pristine CQDs especially for pyrene structures making them more polarizable and easy to be excited, in contrast to coronene structures which underwent a slight variation. From the other hand, decoration mechanism enhances drastically the electrophilicity of pyrene configurations making them much reactive, while very weak reduction is observed for functionalized-coronene structures certifying their high chemical stability, except for
C
24
H
12
CHO which presents a minor energetic stability compared to the other decorated coronene structures, but still presenting a better stability than pyrene configurations, since larger structures are characterized by a better stability than smaller ones. This finding well concords with the previous results reported for the binding energy.

Furthermore, compared to pyrene structure, the coronene structure exhibits shorter zigzag edges and more armchair corners which explain their considerable stability, since the zigzag edges are more reactive than armchair ones according to [51,52], which renders the structures with armchair edges potential to improve stability against chemicals. It follows that, due to their larger size, hexagonal Janus h-C24H12 are more energetically and chemically stable than diamond shaped Janus C
16
H10 nanoparticles in concordance with [53].

The interplay between the shape and surface-functionalization also impacts the electronic properties of pristine CQDs. Table 2 presents the obtained values of HOMO–LUMO (H-L) energy gap at GGA-DFT level in terms of shapes and functional groups type. One can observe the effect of zigzag edges and armchair corners on the variation of the H-L gap energy of the structures. Indeed, the band gap tends to reduce quickly for pyrene configurations which exhibit more pronounced zigzag edges compared to coronene structures having more armchair corners than the pyrene ones. This explains the rapid reduction of the H-L gap in the case of pyrene in good agreement with [34,44]. Furthermore,
C
16
H
10
structures behave oppositely from
C
24
H
12
QDs depending on the employed functional group. Namely, the functionalization drops the H-L gap of the pyrene former with a descending order of the calculated H-L gap as follows CHO >
COOH >
OH >
NH
2
. However, it rises the energy gap of hexagonal coronene C24H12 in the following ascending order OH <
NH2
<
COOH <
CHO. This unexpectable behaviour of the H-L gap concordes well with the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots with oxygen-containing groups [54] and is elucidated to the large uneven hybridization of frontiers molecular orbitals in the corresponding structures which significantly impacts the gap energy.

To reproduce an H-L gap in good agreement with the experiments, we use many-body perturbation theory within Green function and screened Coulomb interaction (GW) approximation which includes excitonic effects given by the quasiparticle excitations. The obtained GW energy gap values summarized in Table 2 are found to be particularly larger than the GGA-DFT values since the generalized gradient approximation is known to describe successfully the ground-state properties and to underestimate the gap energy in semiconductors. Indeed, the corrected GW H-L gap of pristine pyrene and coronene are found to be in good accordance with experimental measurements of 7.41 eV [55] and 6.82 eV [56], respectively, which confirms the reliability of this approximation. Furthermore, the QP corrections enhance the gap energy by about
95 – 98 %
for Janus-
C
16
H
10
QDs and by
55 – 58 %
for
C
24
H
12
configurations as indicated in Table 2. The difference in the increase of gap energy of the two Janus nanoparticles is attributed to the much weaker screening in the smaller size which both concur to improve electrons correlation.

To highlight the atoms contribution and the surface-functionalization effect on the variation of the HOMO–LUMO energy gap, Figs. 2 and 3 depict the charge density distribution of frontier molecular orbitals. For
C
16
H
10
configurations, the functionalization gives rise to new distribution of the charge density of HOMO and LUMO with respect to the pristine structure. This new charge density behaviour is found to be the same for all JCQDs regardless of the functional group type. More explicitly, the seam carbon atoms dominates both the HOMO and the LUMO with an additional contribution originating from the asymmetric functionalization besides a minor contribution originating from either functional groups or hydrogen located in the surface.

Likewise, the charge density distribution corresponding to
C
24
H
12
structures depends strongly on the functional group type. As shown in Fig. 3, the contribution to the charge density for OH and NH2 configurations originates from carbon atoms, with a remarkable contribution of dopants found for HOMO, while the CHO-CQDs includes the contribution of carbon skeleton as well as formyl molecules and hydrogen atoms in both HOMO and LUMO. Finally, a trifle contribution localized mainly on carboxyl molecules for HOMO and carbon skeleton for LUMO is observed for COOH
C
24
H
12
conformer which explains the important enhancement of the HOMO–LUMO gap in this configuration compared to other coronene structures.

The optical properties of JCQDs are also investigated through plotting their absorption spectra at GW + BSE level with the incident light polarized along x -direction (see Fig. 4). Notice that only the effect of the x-polarization is considered in this work since the results are similar for y-polarization direction. The surface-functionalization brings crucial modifications to the optical behaviour of pristine CQDs. Starting with
C
16
H
10
, one can see that the absorption curves underwent a red-shift accompanied by a prominent increment of their intensities in good agreement with results obtained upon modifying the basal plan of C132 QDs with oxygen-containing functional groups (COOH, COC, OH, CHO, and OCH3 ) [54]. While in the case of
C
24
H
12
QDs, except for CHO functionalized structure, a blue-shift is shown for all the configurations accompanied by a remarkable decrement of the absorption intensity as compared to pristine structure. These different behaviours relate to the reordering of the orbitals caused by surface-functionalization and the creation of new hybrid orbitals from mixing the atomic orbitals of pristine QD and functional groups [54]. On the other hand, the decoration of the basal plan has also a considerable effect on the anisotropic behaviour of the optical response shown by pure pyrene configuration. More precisely, surface-functionalization results in suppressing this anisotropic nature of
C
16
H
10
structure giving rise to Janus pyrene structures with an optically isotropic nature. Concerning
C
24
H
12
QDs, the functionalization maintains its isotropic behaviour.

Furthermore, the optical gap
E
o p t
X
and the exciton binding energy
E
b
X
vary in the same way as the H-L gap so that for
C
16
H
10
,
E
o p t
X
and
E
b
X
values undergo a considerable drop while for
C
24
H
12
, the values are slightly enhanced. This finding is elucidated to the creation of hybridization between C-atoms and functional groups thus a larger uneven hybridization results in decreasing the optical gap. Whereas, for
C
24
H
12
, a concurrence between the hybridization mixing the atomic orbitals of both carbon skeleton and functional groups from one side, and the considerable reduced screening caused by the larger quantity of dopants from the other side, induces a shift of
E
b
X
. It follows that the excitonic effects in coronene JCQDs are more significant than pyrene with remarkable first bright exciton binding energy values
E
b
X
larger for functionalized
C
24
H
12
compared to values for Janus-
C
16
H
10
. Consequently,
C
24
H
12
JCQDs represent the configurations with stronger electron–hole interactions.

By comparing the absorption energies
E
o p t
X
, obtained through the BSE method, to the ones
E
a b s
obtained via TDDFT approach, the values sounds approximately close. Obviously, the stokes shift which is the difference between the absorption and the emission energies corresponding to peak maxima of the same electronic transition, indicates the strong photoluminescence characterizing the functionalized structures as compared to pristine ones, especially for coronene configurations (see Table 3). Notably, the emission wavelengths corresponds to the red region of the solar spectrum for
C
16
H
10
OH, the near-infrared (NIR) for
C
16
H
10
COOH and
C
16
H
10
NH2 and the mid-infrared (MIR) for
C
16
H
10
CHO. For coronene counterparts, the emission wavelengths vary from visible for
C
24
H
12
COOH and
C
24
H
12
CHO, passing through NIR for
C
24
H
12
NH2 to far-infrared (FIR) for
C
24
H
12
OH. This variety in photoluminescence wavelengths is highly recommended for a whole range of nanomedical applications.

The photoluminescence character of JCQDs and the excitons population on excited states are explored through the energy splitting between singlet and triplet energy splitting
Δ
S − T
X
. Table 2 lists the obtained
Δ
S − T
X
values which clearly approve that surface-functionalization as well as the shape variation are interesting key factors for engineering efficient QDs for the target applications [57]. More precisely, they adjust and control the exchange splitting of pristine CQDs, namely
1 . 27
eV
for pyrene and
1 . 60
eV
for coronene, which concordes with [58]. These
Δ
S − T
X
values are lowered for
C
16
H
10
and
C
24
H
12
JCQDs, and coronene configurations exhibit the largest splitting values compared to their pyrene counterparts. It follows that one can deduce the photophysical process occurring in JCQDs. Indeed, in
C
16
H
10
QDs, the splitting values highly decrease with respect to pristine
C
16
H
10
which reveals that all these functionalized structures are characterized by a thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). In the case of
C
24
H
12
JCQDs, a conventional fluorescence takes place in all configurations with the exception of
C
24
H
12
OH which is characterized by the TADF process.

On the other hand, the TDDFT calculations of the optical and photoluminescence behaviour, reported in Table 3, confirm the results found by the BSE method, showing that the asymmetric functionalization of pyrene and coronene create a new family of QDs with totally different and promising optical features. More precisely, our studied JQDs are characterized by emission wavelengths with different colours ranging from visible to far infrared regions of the solar spectrum [59].

Finally, all these findings show that our JCQDs could be useful for a whole range of applications, like OLEDs which employs TADF molecules characterized by small splitting values [60,61], and medical nanodevices which require utilizing fluorescent nanostructures with various wavelength values, what prompted us to examine the photoluminescence yield in our structures through TDDFT calculations. Indeed, red- near infrared emissive GQDs are very needed in nanomedical applications since this emitting window is very appealing for nanomedical imaging and especially for visualizing and treating deep organs [62,63]. Furthermore, Janus oxidized graphene quantum dots can be employed in self-propelled cellular translocation which opens up brand-new avenues for developing GQD-enabled nanocarriers or antibiotics.

To summarize the present work, we successfully designed novel Janus graphene derivatives, namely Janus quantum dots via an asymmetric surface functionalization, using hydrogen on one side and some organic functional groups on the opposite side. Subsequently, we investigated their physical properties including the structural characteristics, the energetic and chemical stability and especially the optoelectronic and photoluminescence properties, with density functional theory, time-depending density functional theory, the GW method, and the Bethe–Salpeter equation. Among two different shapes which are the hexagonal and diamond shaped configurations, the former one is found to be the most stable. From the other hand, the reordering of orbitals as well as the creation of new molecular orbitals upon frontier molecular hybridization caused by surface functionalization have a crucial impact on the variation of the energy gap, the optical absorption with a remarkable enhanced intensities and the excitonic binding energy. Indeed, the asymmetric functionalization reduces the HL energy gap for pyrene, while the pristine coronene gap value increased upon function-group use. Additionally, JQDs are found to be highly luminescent comparing to pristine structures as it was confirmed by the singlet–triplet energy splitting and the emission energy. They are also characterized by a variety of photophysical processes, allowing their use in divers fields. All of these insightful characteristics and findings corroborate the crucial significance of JQDs for experimental designs for a wide range of applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the “Académie Hassan II des Sciences et Techniques”-Morocco for its financial support. The authors also thank the LPHE-MS, Faculty of Sciences, Mohammed V University in Rabat, Morocco for the technical support through computer facilities, where all the calculations have been performed.
